stage-0.prompt: A teammate on macOS edits a file in the Desktop app's *Chat* tab and asks it to follow the conventions in the repo's committed CLAUDE.md. Nothing in CLAUDE.md is respected, and the agent can't even see the repo files. Another teammate using the terminal `claude` command on the same repo gets full CLAUDE.md adherence. What is the correct explanation?
stage-0.explanation: The Desktop app has three tabs: Chat, Cowork, and Code. Only Code (and Cowork, remotely) is the Claude Code engine. Chat is the claude.ai experience with no repo access and does not read CLAUDE.md. The right move is to use the Code tab. Distractor 1 wrongly assumes every Desktop surface is the same engine — Chat is the explicit exception. Distractor 3 is false: the Code tab and Cowork both read shared config. Distractor 4 invents a macOS-specific path; CLAUDE.md location does not vary by OS for project scope, and the failure is the tab, not the platform.
options: The Chat tab is a different front-end of the same engine, so it should read CLAUDE.md — there is a config-resolution bug
The Chat tab is the claude.ai-style chat experience with no file access; it is NOT the coding agent and does not read CLAUDE.md, unlike the Code tab
CLAUDE.md is only read by the terminal CLI; GUI front-ends never read it
macOS Desktop reads a different config path, so the committed project CLAUDE.md doesn't apply there

stage-0.prompt: You want to GUARANTEE that the agent can never read `./.env`, regardless of what the model decides in any session. Which mechanisms actually enforce this (as opposed to merely guiding the model)? Select all that apply.
stage-0.explanation: CLAUDE.md is delivered as a user message after the system prompt with 'no guarantee of strict compliance' — it is context, not enforcement, so options 1 and 4 (both CLAUDE.md, just different scopes) only guide. The docs explicitly say: to BLOCK an action regardless of the model's decision, use a PreToolUse hook or permissions.deny. So options 2, 3, and 5 enforce. Note 2 and 5 are both permissions.deny (project vs managed) and both enforce because deny rules merge across scopes and deny wins on conflict. Option 4 is the trap: moving a CLAUDE.md sentence to a broader scope does not change its nature from guidance to enforcement.
options: A line in CLAUDE.md saying 'Never read .env files'
A `permissions.deny` entry like `Read(./.env)` in settings.json
A PreToolUse hook that blocks the read
Putting the instruction in `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` (user scope) so it applies to all projects
Setting the rule in managed-settings.json's `permissions.deny`

stage-0.prompt: A project's committed `.claude/settings.json` has `Bash(npm run build)` in its `allow` list. The org's `managed-settings.json` has `Bash(npm run build)` in its `deny` list. A developer also adds it to `allow` in their gitignored `settings.local.json`. When the developer runs `npm run build`, what happens and why?
stage-0.explanation: Permission rules behave differently from other settings: they MERGE across scopes rather than override, and deny wins on conflict. A managed deny therefore cannot be unlocked by a project or local allow. Distractor 0 misapplies the general precedence ladder (where local DOES outrank project) — but that ladder governs non-permission settings; permission rules use merge+deny-first, and managed sits above everything regardless. Distractor 2 invents an 'ask' fallback that doesn't exist for allow/deny conflict. Distractor 3 treats rules as a vote count, which is not how merge+deny-first works.
options: It runs silently — local scope has higher precedence than managed for permission rules
It is blocked — permission rules merge across scopes and deny wins on conflict, so the managed deny cannot be unlocked by any allow
It prompts the developer to confirm — conflicting allow/deny resolves to 'ask'
It runs silently — two allow entries (project + local) outvote one deny entry

stage-0.prompt: Two CLAUDE.md files in a monorepo give contradictory instructions about the same `src/billing/` directory. A reviewer expects Claude to honor the more specific (deeper) file's rule every time. In practice, behavior is inconsistent across sessions. What does the content say about this?
stage-0.explanation: The docs say conflicts are silent and 'Claude may pick one arbitrarily,' recommending periodic audits and `claudeMdExcludes` in monorepos. Distractor 0 confuses load ORDER (more specific files are concatenated later in context) with guaranteed OVERRIDE — being read last is not the same as winning a direct contradiction. Distractor 2 is wrong: Claude walks up the tree and concatenates all CLAUDE.md files, and nested ones load on demand. Distractor 3 invents an arbitration role for managed policy that the content does not claim.
options: Deeper CLAUDE.md files always override shallower ones, so the inconsistency is a bug to report
On a direct conflict Claude 'may pick one arbitrarily'; you should audit periodically and can use `claudeMdExcludes` to skip other teams' files
Only the file at the repo root is loaded, so the deeper file is being ignored
Conflicts are resolved by the managed-policy CLAUDE.md, which silently arbitrates

stage-0.prompt: Your team standardizes on running BOTH Claude Code and OpenAI Codex. You want a single source of instructions both agents honor. Which statements are accurate per the content? Select all that apply.
stage-0.explanation: The docs state Claude Code reads CLAUDE.md, not AGENTS.md (option 0), and the @-import of AGENTS.md is the canonical bridge so both agents share one source (option 1). Option 3 is the table's description of Codex: a different vendor's tool reading AGENTS.md + ~/.codex/config.toml. The traps: option 4 is the central misconception this stage exists to kill — Codex is NOT a Claude Code front-end and shares NO Claude config. Option 3 being true makes option 2 false: Codex does not read Claude's settings.json or MCP config; it has its own config files.
options: Claude Code reads CLAUDE.md, not AGENTS.md, so Codex's AGENTS.md is not automatically picked up by Claude Code
Adding `@AGENTS.md` as an import inside CLAUDE.md lets both agents share one instruction source
Codex reads Claude Code's `.claude/settings.json` and MCP config, so committing those covers Codex too
Codex is a different vendor's agentic coding tool and reads `AGENTS.md` plus `~/.codex/config.toml`
Codex is just another front-end of the Claude Code engine, so it shares all of Claude's config

stage-0.prompt: A developer wants to try an experimental MCP server and a looser permission just for themselves on the current project, WITHOUT touching the committed team config or affecting teammates. For the permission/settings part, where should it go — and what is the one MCP caveat?
stage-0.explanation: Personal, this-project-only overrides go in `*.local.*` files, which are gitignored — here `settings.local.json`. The stated exception is that MCP's 'local' scope does not live in the repo; it lives in `~/.claude.json`. Distractor 0 is wrong: `.claude/settings.json` is the COMMITTED team file, not gitignored. Distractor 2 wrongly uses user scope, which applies to ALL projects, not just this one. Distractor 3 confuses files: permissions live in settings.json, not CLAUDE.md, and CLAUDE.local.md is for personal instructions, not permission rules.
options: Put both in `.claude/settings.json`; it's already gitignored by default
Put the setting in `.claude/settings.local.json` (gitignored, this-project-only); but MCP's 'local' scope is the exception — it lives in `~/.claude.json`, not in the repo
Put both in `~/.claude/settings.json` so they apply across all projects
Put the setting in `CLAUDE.local.md`, since permissions are configured in CLAUDE.md

stage-0.prompt: A team lead reasons: 'Onboarding the team means writing setup docs for the terminal CLI, separate docs for the Desktop Code tab, and separate docs for Cowork, since they're three products.' Why is this framing wrong according to stage 0?
stage-0.explanation: The load-bearing fact of stage 0: the CLI, Code tab, and Cowork are one engine reading the same shared config (CLAUDE.md, MCP, hooks, skills, settings). Onboarding collapses to authoring and committing config once; the front-end becomes a personal-preference detail. Distractor 0 is false: Cowork is Claude Code (remote), not Codex. Distractor 2 contradicts the core claim that all Claude Code front-ends share config. Distractor 3 is explicitly contradicted — the quickstart says you can run Desktop and CLI simultaneously on the same project.
options: Because Cowork is actually a Codex product and needs OpenAI docs instead
Because the CLI, Code tab, and Cowork are the same Claude Code engine reading the same committed config — you author config files once and every front-end obeys them
Because only the terminal CLI reads committed config; the GUI surfaces require per-user manual setup
Because the Desktop app cannot run on the same project simultaneously as the CLI, so they must be documented as mutually exclusive

stage-1.prompt: A teammate added an MCP server to the legacy Claude desktop chat app's `claude_desktop_config.json` weeks ago. Today they open the Desktop **Code** tab and the server is nowhere to be seen, yet their CLI sessions on the same repo can use a *different* MCP server fine. Which statements correctly explain what's going on? (Select all that apply.)
stage-1.explanation: The Code tab is the same engine as the CLI and reads the same Claude Code config stack (`.mcp.json`, user/project settings, Plugins UI), NOT the legacy chat app's `claude_desktop_config.json` (claims: shared engine/config; legacy config is separate and invisible to Code). Option 2 is the central misconception this stage exists to kill — Code and CLI are one product, not two engines. Option 4 contradicts the verified fact that `claude_desktop_config.json` does not appear in the Code tab; sharing applies to the Claude Code config, not the chat app's file.
options: The Code tab does not read `claude_desktop_config.json`; that file belongs to the separate legacy chat app, so its servers never surface in Code
The Code tab and the CLI read the *same* Claude Code config (`.mcp.json`, user/project settings), which is why the CLI's server works and could appear in Code too
The Code tab is a different engine from the CLI, so it maintains an independent MCP list that happens to exclude the chat app's servers
To expose the server in Code, the teammate should re-add it via Claude Code config (`.mcp.json` / settings) or the Code tab's **+ → Plugins** UI, not via `claude_desktop_config.json`
Because Desktop and CLI share config, anything in `claude_desktop_config.json` is automatically inherited by both surfaces

stage-1.prompt: A CI pipeline runs `claude --bare -p "Summarize CHANGELOG.md"` and the run fails because Claude can't authenticate, even though the developer's laptop runs the same command fine interactively. What is the most likely cause grounded in how `--bare` works?
stage-1.explanation: The docs state bare mode skips OAuth/keychain reads, so Anthropic auth must come from `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` or an `apiKeyHelper` passed via `--settings`. The laptop works because it uses the cached interactive OAuth/keychain credential, which CI lacks. Option 2 is wrong — bare skips *auto-discovery* of config (hooks, skills, MCP, CLAUDE.md), not networking. Option 3 conflates tool permissions with authentication; a missing Read grant aborts a tool use, not auth. Option 4 invents an OS restriction the CLI does not have.
options: Bare mode skips OAuth/keychain reads, so auth must come from `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` or an `apiKeyHelper` in the `--settings` JSON — the CI runner has neither
`--bare` disables network access, so no provider can be reached from CI
Bare mode requires `--allowedTools "Read"` and the missing tool grant is what surfaces as an auth error
`--bare` only works on Linux, and the CI runner is on Windows

stage-1.prompt: You want a headless commit helper and write: `claude -p "..." --allowedTools "Bash(git diff*)"`. A reviewer warns this is subtly wrong. What is the actual problem?
stage-1.explanation: The docs are explicit: the space before `*` is load-bearing. `Bash(git diff *)` allows commands starting with `git diff `, while `Bash(git diff*)` would also match `git diff-index` — an unintended broadening. Option 2 is false; permission-rule syntax is exactly how you scope Bash. Option 3 is false; `--allowedTools` is the mechanism for pre-approving tools headlessly. Option 4 misreads the matching — `git diff *` matches `git diff` followed by anything, and the concern is over-broad, not over-narrow.
options: Without the space before `*`, `Bash(git diff*)` also matches commands like `git diff-index`, broadening permission beyond `git diff`
`--allowedTools` does not accept `Bash(...)` scoping; you must list the whole `Bash` tool unscoped
Headless mode ignores `--allowedTools` entirely, so the scoping has no effect
The pattern is too narrow — `Bash(git diff *)` would block `git diff` with no arguments

stage-1.prompt: Your team is choosing surfaces for different jobs. Which assignments are correct given what each surface can and cannot do? (Select all that apply.)
stage-1.explanation: Each of the first four maps a surface to a capability the content verifies: CLI is the only Linux surface and the only headless one; Cowork is non-coding knowledge work that shows its plan before acting; the Code tab's headline feature is parallel sessions each in its own git worktree with visual diff review; `--bare` skips auto-discovery of hooks/MCP/CLAUDE.md for reproducibility. Option 5 is the trap: the Desktop app is macOS/Windows only and interactive-only — it cannot run on a headless Linux server nor do stdin piping/exit codes. That is the CLI's job.
options: A Linux CI runner that must call the agent non-interactively → the CLI/headless mode, because the Desktop app is not available on Linux
A PM organizing a Drive and building a report, wanting to approve the plan before the agent acts → Cowork
A backend dev who wants several concurrent tasks each isolated in its own git worktree with a visual diff loop → the Desktop Code tab
A reproducible nightly review that must ignore any teammate's `~/.claude` hooks or the repo's `.mcp.json` → `claude --bare -p ...`
Running an interactive REPL with stdin piping and exit codes on a headless Linux server → the Desktop Code tab

stage-1.prompt: A teammate writes a careful `CLAUDE.md` for the repo and is frustrated that the half of the team using OpenAI Codex seems to ignore it entirely. What is the correct explanation and remedy?
stage-1.explanation: Codex is a separate vendor product with its own config tree (`~/.codex`) and reads `AGENTS.md`; Claude Code natively reads `CLAUDE.md` and NOT `AGENTS.md`, and nothing syncs between them — so teams bridge via symlink or a generated mirror. Option 2 wrongly implies Codex reads `CLAUDE.md` at all. Option 3 contradicts the verified claim that nothing syncs and the two have separate config trees. Option 4 reverses the mapping — Claude Code reads `CLAUDE.md`, Codex reads `AGENTS.md`.
options: Codex is a separate OpenAI product that reads `AGENTS.md`, not `CLAUDE.md`; bridge them (symlink or generated mirror) since nothing syncs automatically
Codex reads `CLAUDE.md` but only from `~/.codex`, so the file must be copied there to take effect
Codex shares Claude Code's config tree, so the issue is a stale cache that re-indexing fixes
`CLAUDE.md` is Codex's native file but Claude Code is the one that needs `AGENTS.md`, so the mapping is reversed

stage-1.prompt: In a headless run, you pass `--permission-mode acceptEdits` and the prompt asks Claude to write some files and also run `curl` to post results to an internal webhook. The file writes succeed but the `curl` aborts the run. Why?
stage-1.explanation: Per the content, `acceptEdits` auto-writes files and auto-approves common fs commands (mkdir, touch, mv, cp), but other shell/network calls still need an `--allowedTools` entry — so `curl` aborts while edits succeed. Option 2 misstates the mode (it auto-applies edits, not just reads). Option 3 describes `dontAsk` (deny anything not in `permissions.allow` or the read-only set), not `acceptEdits`. Option 4 is false — `curl` would run if explicitly allowed via `--allowedTools`.
options: `acceptEdits` auto-approves file edits and common fs commands (mkdir, touch, mv, cp), but other shell/network calls like `curl` still need an explicit `--allowedTools` entry
`acceptEdits` only auto-approves reads, so even the file writes should have failed — the success is a bug
`acceptEdits` denies anything not in `permissions.allow`, which is why `curl` was blocked
Network calls are never permitted in any headless mode regardless of flags

stage-1.prompt: A scripted caller runs `claude -p "..." --output-format json` and wants per-invocation spend without opening a dashboard. Which statement is accurate about what they get?
stage-1.explanation: The content states the `json` output includes `total_cost_usd` and a per-model cost breakdown, letting a scripted caller track spend per invocation. Option 2 is wrong — `stream-json` is for newline-delimited live events, not a prerequisite for cost. Option 3 confuses `--json-schema` (which constrains output into the `structured_output` field) with cost reporting, which comes with `json` regardless. Option 4 contradicts the verified headless cost capability.
options: The `json` payload includes `total_cost_usd` and a per-model cost breakdown, so the script can track spend per call directly
Cost data requires `--output-format stream-json`; plain `json` omits it
You must add `--json-schema` to receive any cost fields
Cost is only available in the interactive `/config` view, not in headless output

stage-2.prompt: Your project's committed `.claude/settings.json` has `permissions.allow: ["Bash(git push:*)"]`. A teammate's user-scope `~/.claude/settings.json` has `permissions.deny: ["Bash(git push:*)"]`. When that teammate runs the agent in your repo and it tries `git push`, what happens, and why?
stage-2.explanation: Permission arrays MERGE (concatenate) across scopes, and rules evaluate deny → ask → allow with first-match-wins, so the matching deny is reached before any allow regardless of which scope authored it — a higher-scope deny is a floor no lower scope can relax. Option A invokes a 'more specific wins' rule that applies to CLAUDE.md/settings *keys*, not to permission rule evaluation (where deny simply wins). Option C is wrong because scopes don't replace each other — the arrays concatenate, so the project allow still exists, it's just never reached. Option D invents a 'conflict → ask' fallback that the deny-first ordering makes impossible.
options: It's allowed — project scope is more specific than user scope, so the project allow wins.
It's denied — the allow/ask/deny arrays merge across scopes and evaluation is deny → ask → allow, so the user-scope deny is a floor the project allow cannot relax.
It's denied — when allow and deny conflict, the lower (project) scope is dropped entirely and only the user scope applies.
It prompts the user — a direct allow/deny conflict across scopes always falls back to 'ask'.

stage-2.prompt: A teammate switches from Claude Code to Codex (same VS Code, same repo) and suddenly the agent ignores the team's conventions. The repo has a well-maintained `CLAUDE.md` and no `AGENTS.md`. What is the most likely root cause?
stage-2.explanation: The asymmetry is the whole point of the stage: Codex reads `AGENTS.md`; Claude Code reads `CLAUDE.md` and does NOT read `AGENTS.md` even as a fallback — and the relationship is symmetric, Codex does not read `CLAUDE.md`. With only a `CLAUDE.md` present, Codex sees no project doc. Option B invents a non-existent Codex→CLAUDE.md fallback (and the 200-line figure is the CLAUDE.md adherence target, not a Codex truncation; Codex's cap is 32 KiB on AGENTS.md). Option C's trust dialog gates `.claude/` skills/hooks/settings, not markdown instruction discovery. Option D is false — conventions are markdown, and settings.json is for permissions/hooks/env.
options: Codex reads `AGENTS.md`, not `CLAUDE.md`, and Claude Code reads `CLAUDE.md`, not `AGENTS.md` — with no `AGENTS.md` (or bridge), Codex loaded no project instructions.
Codex falls back to `CLAUDE.md` when `AGENTS.md` is absent, but only reads the first 200 lines, so the conventions were truncated.
The trust dialog wasn't accepted, so Codex couldn't load the committed `.claude/` directory.
Codex requires the conventions to live in `.claude/settings.json`, not in a markdown file.

stage-2.prompt: Your CLAUDE.md says 'API handlers validate with Pydantic and log all state changes,' and your `.claude/settings.json` denies `Bash(git push:*)`. Which statements are TRUE about the difference between these two mechanisms? (Select all that apply.)
stage-2.explanation: Per the docs quoted in the stage: settings rules are enforced by the client regardless of what Claude decides, while CLAUDE.md content is delivered as a user message after the system prompt and merely shapes behavior with no compliance guarantee — so A, B, and D are correct (only the deny gives a real guarantee). C is the central misconception the stage targets: CLAUDE.md is explicitly NOT a hard enforcement layer. E is wrong because CLAUDE.md cannot override a client-enforced permission rule — instructions don't relax settings.
options: The settings deny is enforced by the client regardless of what the agent decides; the CLAUDE.md rule only shapes behavior with no guarantee of compliance.
The CLAUDE.md rule is delivered as a user message after the system prompt, so the agent reads it and tries to follow it but may not.
Both are hard enforcement layers; the only difference is where the file lives.
If you need a guarantee that `git push` never runs, the deny rule provides it; rewording the CLAUDE.md line more forcefully would not.
The settings deny will be ignored if a CLAUDE.md line elsewhere says 'always push when done.'

stage-2.prompt: A root `CLAUDE.md` says 'use tabs.' A `CLAUDE.md` in `src/api/` says 'use 2-space indentation.' The agent is editing a file in `src/api/`. Per the documented load behavior, which best describes what the agent sees?
stage-2.explanation: Subdirectory CLAUDE.md files load on demand when Claude reads files there (the context cascade), and content is concatenated root-first so the nearer file is read last. But the docs warn that *contradictory* rules across nested files make Claude 'pick one arbitrarily' — later position is not a guaranteed clean override, so the right move is to fix the contradiction. A is wrong: nested files DO load on demand. C is wrong: concatenation appends, it doesn't replace/remove the root content. D is wrong: subdirectory files auto-load on file access; only `@`-imports require the import syntax.
options: Only the root file loads at session start; the `src/api/` file is ignored because nested files don't load.

Both load, but because Claude concatenates root-first (root read first, files closer to cwd read last) the `src/api/` rule appears later — the docs note that contradictory nested rules cause Claude to 'pick one arbitrarily,' so this is a content bug to resolve, not a clean override.
The `src/api/` file fully replaces the root file, so 'use tabs' is removed from context.
Neither loads until you explicitly @-import them.

stage-2.prompt: A new teammate clones the repo and accepts the trust dialog. Which of the following do they get FROM the committed `.claude/` directory traveling with the repo? (Select all that apply.)
stage-2.explanation: A committed `.claude/` directory carries the project skills, agents, hooks, and `settings.json` to anyone who clones and accepts the trust dialog — so A, B, and C are correct. D is wrong because `settings.local.json` is per-developer and gitignored; it doesn't travel and isn't 'the teammate's' until they create their own. E is wrong because managed-policy CLAUDE.md lives in a system path (`/etc/claude-code/` on Linux) deployed by IT machine-wide and cannot be excluded — it does not ship inside the repo's `.claude/`.
options: Project skills
Project agents and hooks
The team `settings.json` (permissions, hooks, env)
Their personal overrides from `.claude/settings.local.json`
The managed-policy CLAUDE.md that IT deploys to `/etc/claude-code/`

stage-2.prompt: A mixed Windows/macOS team wants Claude Code and Codex to read identical project rules, and Windows developers do not have admin rights or Developer Mode. They also occasionally want a Claude-only note (e.g., 'use plan mode under src/billing/'). Which bridge should they choose?
stage-2.explanation: The `@AGENTS.md` import is the recommended portable bridge: it works on Windows with no admin rights, recurses up to four hops, AND lets you append engine-specific (Claude-only) notes below the import — exactly this team's two requirements. Option A fails both: the symlink needs Administrator/Developer Mode on Windows (and `core.symlinks`, else it checks out as a plain text file), and identical bytes means no place for a Claude-only note. Option C is the hand-maintained-divergence anti-pattern the whole stage warns against. Option D is wrong: `settings.json` is Claude Code permissions/hooks/env — Codex doesn't read it, and prose conventions aren't permission rules.
options: `ln -s AGENTS.md CLAUDE.md` symlink — it guarantees identical bytes with zero drift risk.
A first-line `@AGENTS.md` import in `CLAUDE.md`, with Claude-specific notes below it.
Maintain `CLAUDE.md` and `AGENTS.md` as two independently hand-edited files and diff them weekly.
Put the rules only in `.claude/settings.json` so both engines enforce them.

stage-2.prompt: Your team adopts a generated `AGENTS.md` rendered from `CLAUDE.md`. A reviewer edits `AGENTS.md` directly and opens a PR; CI's `render_agents_md.py --check` step fails. What does this failure actually mean, and what's the correct fix?
stage-2.explanation: `--check` mode re-renders the projection and exits non-zero if it differs from the committed file (same pattern as `black --check`). Editing the derived `AGENTS.md` by hand is exactly what it catches: the rule is 'exactly one file is authored by hand; the other is derived,' so you fix the canonical `CLAUDE.md` and regenerate. Option A misreads a content-diff failure as a tooling/version bug. Option C confuses the drift check with Codex's 32 KiB instruction cap — unrelated. Option D's 'manually re-sync the derived file' reintroduces the second hand-authored authority the projection exists to eliminate.
options: The renderer is broken; pin the Python version and the check will pass.
The committed `AGENTS.md` no longer matches what re-rendering from the canonical `CLAUDE.md` produces — the fix is to edit the canonical source and regenerate, not to hand-edit the derived file.
`AGENTS.md` exceeded the 32 KiB `project_doc_max_bytes` cap and must be truncated.
Both files drifted, so the fix is to manually re-sync `AGENTS.md` to match `CLAUDE.md` byte-for-byte.

stage-3.prompt: A teammate writes a non-negotiable rule into CLAUDE.md: "Never force-push to main." Two weeks later, under a long, context-heavy session, Claude force-pushes to main anyway. Per the content, what is the correct diagnosis and fix?
stage-3.explanation: The content explicitly names this failure mode: encoding a non-negotiable policy as advisory CLAUDE.md prose, which 'gets ignored under load.' A rule that must ALWAYS hold regardless of the model's choice maps to a hook — deterministic, event-driven, can hard-block (exit 2 / deny). Option B is wrong: skills are model-INVOKED (advisory, and only when the description matches), so they are weaker than prose for enforcement, not stronger. Option C misapplies progressive disclosure — that mechanism governs SKILL.md descriptions/budgets, not CLAUDE.md facts, and isn't why the rule was ignored. Option D is wrong: sub-agent tool restrictions bound what a spawned worker can do within its bounded mission; they don't deterministically govern what the main agent does on an arbitrary event.
options: CLAUDE.md prose is advisory and can be ignored under load; the enforcement-grade mechanism is a hook, which fires deterministically on the event and can hard-block (exit 2 / deny)
The rule was placed in the wrong file; moving it from CLAUDE.md into a SKILL.md description would make it always enforced
The CLAUDE.md was too long, so the rule got dropped by progressive disclosure; trimming the file restores enforcement
A sub-agent should own the force-push, since its least-privilege tool restrictions would have blocked the push deterministically

stage-3.prompt: You add a skill with this frontmatter: `disable-model-invocation: true`. You expected Claude to start auto-running it whenever relevant, but it never fires on its own — only when you type the slash command. Why?
stage-3.explanation: `disable-model-invocation: true` is documented to make the skill user-only (/name), and notably its description is 'not even loaded into context' — so the model can never match and auto-fire it. That is the intended behavior for side-effecting workflows like /deploy. B describes a real cap but truncation of an over-long description does not zero it out to disable matching. C confuses the 500-line body recommendation (a per-turn token-cost guideline) with discovery; names are always listed and the body isn't the discovery surface. D describes a portability/drift gotcha, not the effect of this frontmatter flag.
options: `disable-model-invocation: true` means only you can invoke it via /name, and its description is not even loaded into context, so the model has nothing to match on
The description was over the 1,536-char cap, so it was truncated to empty and model invocation silently failed
The skill body exceeded 500 lines, so progressive disclosure dropped it from the discovery tier
The skill was copied rather than symlinked, so it drifted out of the watched directory and stopped being model-invoked

stage-3.prompt: Select ALL statements that are TRUE about how progressive disclosure governs token cost across a session.
stage-3.explanation: A, B, C, and E are stated directly: discovery = name+description; activation loads the full body once and keeps it for the session (no re-read); bundled references load lazily; and on budget overflow the least-used descriptions are dropped first and silently. D is the trap — the content explicitly says the activated body is a 'recurring per-turn token cost' that 'just sits in context,' which is exactly WHY you keep it lean. Believing the body is unloaded after the triggering turn is the common misconception this catches.
options: At discovery, only each skill's name + description is in context; the model matches requests against these
On activation, the full SKILL.md enters context as a message and stays for the rest of the session — Claude Code does not re-read it on later turns
A bundled reference.md is loaded only when Claude actually opens it, so a 4,000-line spec beside a 60-line SKILL.md costs nothing until it's relevant
An activated skill body imposes no recurring cost because it is unloaded after the turn that triggered it
When the description listing budget overflows, the least-used skills' descriptions are dropped first, silently

stage-3.prompt: Your team uses both Claude Code and Codex in VS Code and wants 'configure once.' Per the content, which of the following are TRUE about portable skills and cross-tool config?
stage-3.explanation: A, B, D, E are all stated: shared SKILL.md format but per-tool dirs with no shared resolver; portability = the common subset (Claude-only fields like context: fork / allowed-tools / paths don't port); canonical source projected into each dir with a CI drift check; and projecting AGENTS.md from CLAUDE.md so a dev gets identical rules in either tool. C is the misconception — there is NO shared resolver, so a file in .claude/skills is invisible to Codex; you must mirror it into .agents/skills.
options: Both tools build on the open SKILL.md standard, but each reads only its own skills dir (Claude .claude/skills, Codex .agents/skills) — there is no shared resolver
A portable skill is written in the common frontmatter subset (name + description + body); Claude-only fields like context: fork, allowed-tools, and paths are ignored by Codex
Because the standard is shared, a single SKILL.md placed in .claude/skills is automatically discovered by Codex too
The robust pattern is one canonical source (e.g. ~/.agents/skills) projected into each tool's dir via symlink/copy, with a CI drift check that fails on divergence
Generating-or-symlinking AGENTS.md from CLAUDE.md is part of making the same rules span both tools

stage-3.prompt: A teammate insists: "Skills are the best way to give the agent knowledge that is ALWAYS true, like our coding standards — just put it in a SKILL.md so it loads on demand and saves context." Which response is best supported by the content?
stage-3.explanation: The content states two distinct things: (1) for supplying always-true knowledge, a static docs-index (AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md) currently outperforms model-invoked skills in published evals, and (2) the skill decision rule is about repeated PROCEDURES, not facts ('a fact belongs in CLAUDE.md; a procedure belongs in a skill'). B inverts the eval finding and misuses progressive disclosure as a blanket cost argument. C is wrong — sub-agents return only a summary and are for bounded missions, not durable shared knowledge. D is false; they are different mechanisms with different loading behavior.
options: For always-true knowledge, a static docs-index (AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md) currently outperforms model-invoked skills in published evals; skills are for procedures you keep re-pasting, loaded only when relevant
Agreed — skills always beat a static file because progressive disclosure makes them strictly cheaper than CLAUDE.md
Knowledge belongs in a sub-agent's system prompt, since that's the only place facts persist across the session
It doesn't matter which you pick; CLAUDE.md and SKILL.md are merged into the same context at session start

stage-3.prompt: You want a codebase exploration task that reads ~40 files and runs many greps WITHOUT flooding your main window, and you must guarantee it cannot edit anything. Which configuration matches the content's guidance, and what is the key reason?
stage-3.explanation: This is the canonical sub-agent use case: own context window, returns only a summary (keeping the transcript out of main context), with a least-privilege tools allowlist that the content calls 'enforcement, not a suggestion.' B is wrong because allowed-tools only PRE-APPROVES tools (skips the permission prompt) and does NOT restrict the pool; disallowed-tools removes them. C is wrong: hooks enforce rules but don't give you context isolation — that's the sub-agent's job. D is the inversion trap — omitting `tools` makes the sub-agent inherit ALL tools, the opposite of least-privilege.
options: A sub-agent with `tools: Read, Grep, Glob` (an allowlist) — it runs in its own context, returns only a summary, and the tool allowlist is enforcement, not a suggestion
A skill with `allowed-tools: Read, Grep, Glob` — this removes Write/Edit from the pool while the skill is active
A hook on PreToolUse that denies the request — hooks are the only way to keep search output out of the main context window
A sub-agent with no `tools` field — omitting it gives the safest least-privilege default by inheriting nothing

stage-3.prompt: In a session, your /deploy skill clearly fires (you see it trigger) but the actual deployment behavior seems unchanged from before you added it. Per the content, what does this most likely mean and what's the prescribed next step?
stage-3.explanation: The content's 'triggering vs working' gotcha states exactly this: seeing a skill fire only tells you the description matched, not that the body influenced behavior — the model may simply be choosing other tools. The prescribed remedy is a baseline comparison (enabled vs disabled in fresh sessions) and, for true enforcement, escalating to a hook. B over-claims (firing ≠ body did its job). C confuses the 1,536-char cap, which applies to description+when_to_use in the LISTING, not the body. D is contradicted by the premise — the skill DID fire, so it loaded and /deploy exists; malformed YAML would instead leave empty metadata so model-invocation never fires.
options: Triggering only proves the description matched, not that the body did its job; run the docs' baseline A/B comparison (same prompts, skill enabled vs disabled) — and if it must be enforced, escalate to a hook
Firing proves the body executed; the deploy script itself must be broken, so the skill is working as designed
The body was truncated by the 1,536-char description cap, so re-writing the description fixes the behavior
The skill silently failed to load because of malformed YAML; running claude --debug would show /deploy does not exist

stage-4.prompt: Maya packages her review skill as a plugin named code-review and publishes it. A teammate installs it, then types /review and gets "command not found," but /code-review:review works. Maya, however, can still type /review in her own session. What best explains the difference?
stage-4.explanation: Plugin skills are ALWAYS invoked as /plugin-name:skill-name; only standalone .claude/ skills get short names. Maya still has her original standalone copy, so /review works for her. B is wrong: a misplaced skills/ dir breaks the plugin entirely, not just the short name, and the docs' migration step is to DELETE the standalone original. C invents a renaming behavior; /reload-plugins reports counts but never converts namespacing. D is backwards: disable-model-invocation makes a skill ONLY explicitly invokable, it adds no short alias.
options: Plugin skills are always namespaced as /plugin-name:skill-name, so the installed plugin only exposes /code-review:review; Maya still has her original standalone .claude/ skill, which gets the short name /review
The teammate's install is corrupted because skills/ was placed inside .claude-plugin/; reinstalling will restore the short /review name
Plugin skills get short names only after the first /reload-plugins, which the teammate hasn't run yet
The teammate needs to set disable-model-invocation: true to unlock the short-name alias

stage-4.prompt: You're authoring the code-review plugin. Select ALL items that are in their CORRECT location per the docs.
stage-4.explanation: Only plugin.json goes inside .claude-plugin/. All component directories (skills/, hooks/, agents/) and .mcp.json/.lsp.json live at the plugin ROOT. So plugin.json (correct), hooks/hooks.json at root (correct), and .mcp.json at root (correct) are right. The docs flag exactly the two wrong options with a Warning: putting skills/ or hooks/ INSIDE .claude-plugin/ is the single most common authoring error and prevents components from loading.
options: .claude-plugin/plugin.json
.claude-plugin/skills/review/SKILL.md
hooks/hooks.json at the plugin root
.mcp.json at the plugin root
.claude-plugin/hooks/hooks.json

stage-4.prompt: A plugin's hook script needs to call a helper at scripts/lint.sh inside the plugin, plus keep a SQLite cache that must survive plugin updates. The author hardcodes an absolute path to scripts/lint.sh on their own machine and writes the cache next to it. After a teammate installs and later updates the plugin, both break. Which fix matches the docs?
stage-4.explanation: Plugins are copied to a cache, so author-machine absolute paths and relative paths like ../ won't resolve; ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT} is the correct way to reference files inside the plugin. But the cache directory is REPLACED on update, so state that must survive updates belongs in ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}. B fails because PLUGIN_ROOT is wiped on update, losing the cache. C still uses a relative path that won't resolve. D wrongly stores the script under DATA; the script ships with the plugin and belongs under ROOT.
options: Use ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/lint.sh for the script path, and put the SQLite cache under ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA} so it survives updates
Use ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT} for both the script and the cache, since it always resolves to the installed plugin location
Use a relative path ../scripts/lint.sh and store the cache in ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}
Store both under ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}, the only variable that resolves after install

stage-4.prompt: Your team adds your private marketplace with /plugin marketplace add. Select ALL statements that are TRUE about what happens next.
stage-4.explanation: Adding a marketplace is 'like adding an app store' — it gives a catalog but installs nothing (A true; D false). Installing a plugin activates skills, hooks, and MCP servers as code under one consent click (B true) — this is why audits matter. Each user can register only one marketplace per name; a same-name add replaces the first (C true). Names like claude-community and anthropic-* are RESERVED for Anthropic and rejected for third parties (E false).
options: Adding the marketplace installs none of its plugins; each must be installed individually with /plugin install
Installing a plugin activates its skills, hooks, and MCP servers as code under a single trust decision
If a teammate adds a second marketplace using the same name value, it replaces the first registration for that user
Adding the marketplace automatically enables every plugin listed in marketplace.json
You may name your marketplace claude-community to signal it's open to the team

stage-4.prompt: You set version to 1.2.0 in plugin.json, push three more commits fixing typos without bumping version, then ask why teammates didn't get the fixes. Separately, a colleague's git-hosted plugin with NO version field shows every teammate getting an update on each commit. What accounts for both behaviors?
stage-4.explanation: Per the manifest table: if version is set, users get updates only when you bump it; if omitted on a git-hosted plugin, the commit SHA becomes the version so every commit is a new version. This exactly explains both observations. B is wrong — version DOES gate update delivery. C is wrong — version is not merely a display label; it drives version-based updates. D confuses /reload-plugins (a local dev-iteration reload off disk) with marketplace update delivery, and version is not a sort key.
options: With version set, users only get updates when you bump it; with version omitted on a git-hosted plugin, the commit SHA is used as the version, so every commit counts as a new version
Updates are pull-based via /plugin marketplace update regardless of version, so both should behave identically; the difference is a caching bug
version only affects the display label in the plugin manager and never gates updates
Teammates must run /reload-plugins to receive any update; version controls only sort order in the catalog

stage-4.prompt: While iterating you run claude --plugin-dir ./code-review on a plugin whose name matches one already installed from the marketplace. You expected your local edits to take effect, but they don't — the installed version keeps running. What is the most likely cause consistent with the docs?
stage-4.explanation: Normally a --plugin-dir plugin sharing a name with an installed one wins for that session — letting you test a patch without uninstalling. The documented EXCEPTION is plugins force-enabled/disabled by managed settings, which can't be overridden this way, matching the symptom. B contradicts the 'local copy wins' rule. C is false: --plugin-dir accepts a directory (and also a .zip in v2.1.128+). D confuses version-gating of marketplace updates with local --plugin-dir loading, which reflects disk edits after /reload-plugins.
options: This plugin is force-enabled (or disabled) by managed settings, which --plugin-dir cannot override; normally the local copy would win for the session
--plugin-dir only loads plugins that are NOT already installed; you must uninstall the marketplace copy first
--plugin-dir requires a .zip archive; a plain directory is ignored
Local edits never apply until you bump the version in plugin.json

stage-4.prompt: Validation and pre-publish checks. Select ALL TRUE statements.
stage-4.explanation: A malformed hooks.json blocks the whole plugin from loading (A true) — which is why validation matters. On a marketplace dir the validator checks marketplace.json only (B true); on a plugin dir it checks plugin.json plus component frontmatter and hooks.json JSON (D true). Project/user .claude/agents/ override same-named plugin agents, so leaving originals causes silent shadowing (C true) — hence the docs say delete the originals. E is false: a missing LSP binary yields 'Executable not found in $PATH' in the Errors tab; there is no bundled fallback.
options: A malformed hooks/hooks.json prevents the ENTIRE plugin from loading, not just the hook
claude plugin validate pointed at a marketplace directory checks marketplace.json only — schema, duplicate plugin names, path traversal, and version mismatches
If you migrate a skill into a plugin but leave the same-named original in project/user .claude/agents/, the standalone definition silently overrides the plugin's
claude plugin validate pointed at a plugin directory checks plugin.json plus the frontmatter of skills, agents, and commands and the JSON of hooks.json
An LSP server whose binary is missing from $PATH still loads, falling back to a bundled binary

stage-5.prompt:
A team commits this to their repo's `.claude/settings.json`:
```json
{"permissions":{"allow":["Read(./**)"]}}
```
The org has deployed managed settings containing `"deny": ["Read(./.env.*)"]`. A developer launches Claude Code with `--allowedTools 'Read(./.env.production)'` and asks Claude to read `./.env.production`. What happens, and why?
stage-5.explanation: Permission arrays MERGE (concatenate) across all five scopes, then evaluate deny → ask → allow with first-match-wins, and specificity does NOT change order. The merged deny contains `Read(./.env.*)`, which matches `./.env.production`, so it is denied before allow is ever consulted. Option 2 invokes a real misconception (CLI rank 2 beats project) but rank only governs scalar overrides — for permission rules the docs are explicit that a managed deny cannot be overridden by `--allowedTools`. Option 3 confuses precedence ranks with merge behavior and falsely claims specificity matters. Option 4 invents an 'ask on conflict' fallback that does not exist; deny is absolute.
options: The read is DENIED. Deny rules merge across all scopes and are evaluated before any allow; the managed deny is a floor that neither the project allow nor the CLI allow flag can lift.
The read is ALLOWED. The CLI `--allowedTools` flag is rank 2 (above the project file), and a more specific allow beats a broader deny.
The read is ALLOWED. The project's `Read(./**)` is more recently written than the managed deny, and project settings win on file reads.
The read prompts the user, because conflicting allow and deny rules across scopes always fall back to asking.

stage-5.prompt: You write `deny: ["Bash(aws *)"]` and `allow: ["Bash(aws s3 ls)"]` in the same managed settings file, intending to block all AWS commands except the safe `aws s3 ls`. Claude tries `aws s3 ls`. What happens?
stage-5.explanation: The docs state a broad deny like `Bash(aws *)` blocks every matching call including ones that also match a narrower allow, so a deny rule cannot carry allowlist exceptions. The correct pattern is to invert: allow the safe specifics and use a narrow deny or PreToolUse hook for the dangerous ones. Option 2 is the exact misconception the docs warn against (specificity does not change order). Options 3 and 4 invent 'cancel out' / 'same-scope ask' behaviors that do not exist — first-match-wins is deterministic.
options: It is DENIED — a deny rule cannot carry allowlist exceptions; `Bash(aws *)` matches `aws s3 ls` and deny is evaluated first regardless of the narrower allow.
It is ALLOWED — `Bash(aws s3 ls)` is the more specific pattern, and specificity wins over a broad deny.
It is ALLOWED — allow and deny in the *same* file cancel out for the overlapping command, falling through to the default ask behavior.
It prompts the user, because the two rules conflict within one scope and same-scope conflicts always ask.

stage-5.prompt: Your org wants secret-read denials and destructive-command denials to be UN-relaxable by any developer. Which placements/mechanisms actually guarantee that? (Select all that apply.)
stage-5.explanation: Only managed settings sit at rank 1 above everything a developer controls, and the floor property comes from BOTH array-merge AND deny-first evaluation (options 1 and 4 together state the mechanism). Option 2 is a misconception: committed `.claude/settings.json` is rank 4 and editable by anyone with commit access, so it is a default not a control. Option 3 is the load-bearing distinction of the whole stage — CLAUDE.md shapes what Claude *tries*, not what the harness *allows*; rules are enforced by Claude Code, not the model. Option 5 conflates the autonomy dial with access rules: `plan` mode is orthogonal, can be exited with Shift+Tab, and is not a deny floor.
options: Put the deny rules in managed settings (`/etc/claude-code/managed-settings.json` on Linux/WSL) — rank 1, and a managed deny cannot be overridden by a CLI flag, project, local, or user scope.
Put the deny rules in the committed `.claude/settings.json` so they ship with the repo — since the repo is the team's shared source of truth, this is equally binding.
Rely on a strongly worded `CLAUDE.md` instruction like 'never read .env' — Claude reads CLAUDE.md on every session and will comply.
Use managed settings because deny arrays merge across scopes AND deny is evaluated before allow, so a managed deny is a floor no lower scope can lift.
Set a managed `defaultMode` so the agent is in `plan` mode and therefore cannot run destructive commands.

stage-5.prompt: A developer is in `bypassPermissions` mode (started with `--dangerously-skip-permissions`). Which of the following STILL stop or gate an action in that mode? (Select all that apply.)
stage-5.explanation: In every mode including bypassPermissions, explicit `ask` rules and `deny` rules still apply (options 1, 2). Allow rules (option 3) have NO effect in bypassPermissions because everything is already approved — they are a distractor that sounds protective but isn't. Option 4 is the v2.1.126 change: bypassPermissions now EXECUTES writes to protected paths like `.git`/`.claude` that earlier versions prompted for — so the protected-path check no longer stops it. Option 5 is correct: `rm -rf /` and `rm -rf ~` are among the few things that still stop bypass mode.
options: An explicit `ask` rule (e.g. `ask: ["Bash(git push *)"]`).
A `deny` rule (e.g. `deny: ["Read(./.env.*)"]`).
An `allow` rule that pre-approves a specific command.
The protected-paths safety check that normally blocks writes to `.git/` and `.claude/` (on v2.1.126+).
`rm -rf /` and `rm -rf ~`.

stage-5.prompt: A team adds `{"permissions":{"defaultMode":"auto"}}` to their repo's committed `.claude/settings.json` so every checkout runs hands-off. On v2.1.142+, a teammate clones, starts Claude Code, and finds it still pausing to ask. What is the cause?
stage-5.explanation: v2.1.142 specifically makes Claude Code ignore `auto` set in project or local settings so a repo cannot grant itself auto mode; it must live in user or managed settings. Option 2 invents a deprecation that didn't happen. Option 3 is wrong: `--permission-mode auto` would work, but the question asks why the project setting failed — and even via flag, `auto` is admin-gated/account-qualified, not merely a cycle starting point. Option 4 misattributes the failure to generic precedence; the actual rule is a targeted ignore of `auto` specifically from project/local scope, not a user-override of an otherwise-valid setting.
options: As of v2.1.142, `defaultMode: "auto"` is IGNORED when set in `.claude/settings.json` or `.claude/settings.local.json` — a repository cannot grant itself auto mode; it must come from `~/.claude/settings.json` or managed settings.
`auto` mode was deprecated in v2.1.142 and replaced by `acceptEdits`, which still prompts for shell commands.
The teammate needs to also pass `--permission-mode auto` on the command line; `defaultMode` only sets the Shift+Tab cycle starting point.
Project settings are rank 4 and were overridden by the teammate's user settings, which defaulted them back to `default` mode.

stage-5.prompt: In `acceptEdits` mode, Claude attempts to write to `.git/hooks/pre-commit`. The team has `allow: ["Bash(*)", "Edit(./**)"]` in settings. What happens?
stage-5.explanation: Protected paths (including `.git` and `.claude`) are never auto-approved in any mode except bypassPermissions, and crucially a `permissions.allow` rule does NOT pre-approve them because the safety check runs before allow rules. In a prompting mode like acceptEdits the user gets a prompt. Option 2 is the central misconception — that a broad `Edit(./**)` allow covers protected paths. Option 3 over-generalizes 'denied': in auto mode they route to the classifier and in prompting modes you get a prompt, so it's not always a silent deny. Option 4 is wrong about which modes protect: protected paths are gated in every mode except bypassPermissions, not only auto/dontAsk.
options: The write is NOT auto-approved — `.git` is a protected path, and the protected-path safety check runs BEFORE allow rules, so in a prompting mode like acceptEdits the user gets a prompt regardless of the broad allow.
The write is auto-approved — `Edit(./**)` matches the path and acceptEdits auto-approves all in-scope edits.
The write is silently denied — protected paths are always denied outside bypassPermissions.
The write is auto-approved — `.git` is only protected in `auto` and `dontAsk` modes, not in acceptEdits.

stage-5.prompt: Which statements about Claude Code seats and plan gating are accurate? (Select all that apply.)
stage-5.explanation: Claude Code ships on every Team seat (Standard and Premium); Premium only adds usage and is not the access gate (options 1, 2). The Premium/Code-seat access requirement applies only to legacy Enterprise (option 5), which directly contradicts option 3 — option 3 is the budgeting misconception (paying for Premium to 'unlock' Claude Code) that the stage warns against. Option 4 is correctly hedged: computer use and phone Dispatch are a Pro/Max research preview, and Team/Enterprise exclusion is an inference, not a documented exclusion — so claiming it as a stated fact would be wrong, but stating it as an inference is accurate.
options: Claude Code is included on every Team seat — both Standard and Premium — so Premium is not the access gate for Claude Code.
Premium Team seats buy more usage, not access to Claude Code itself.
A Premium (or Code) seat is required to use Claude Code on a Team plan; Standard seats are excluded.
Computer use and phone Dispatch are a Pro/Max research preview; the docs do not document Team/Enterprise exclusion, so 'not on Team' is an inference rather than a stated fact.
A Premium/Code-seat requirement to access Claude Code applies only to legacy Enterprise, not to current Team plans.

stage-6.prompt: An engineer adds `"Bash(git push:*)": allow` to their repo's `.claude/settings.local.json` (Local scope) to skip the team's `ask` prompt on push. The team's committed `.claude/settings.json` has `git push` under `ask`, and org-wide managed settings have a `deny` on `Bash(git push:*)`. What happens when the engineer runs a git push?
stage-6.explanation: Permission rules (allow/ask/deny) MERGE across every scope rather than override, and a `deny` from any tier always applies — so the managed `deny` blocks the push. Distractor 1 confuses the general settings precedence (Local > Project) with permission behavior; permissions are the explicit exception that merges. Distractor 3 inverts the merge rule. Distractor 4 invents a false scope limit on managed settings, which can enforce permission guardrails.
options: It is allowed — Local scope outranks Project, so the engineer's local `allow` wins.
It is denied — permission rules merge across all scopes and any `deny` (including the managed one) applies regardless of precedence.
It prompts (ask) — the committed Project `ask` rule takes precedence over Local for permission rules.
It is allowed — managed settings only control MCP servers and login org, not Bash permissions.

stage-6.prompt: Your team ships an internal `quality-tools` plugin that iterates daily. `plugin.json` contains `"version": "1.0.0"` (set months ago) and the git source is a GitHub repo on branch `main`. You push 30 new commits over a month but never touch `plugin.json`. A teammate runs `/plugin update`. What do they get?
stage-6.explanation: Version resolution order is (1) `plugin.json` version, (2) marketplace-entry version, (3) git SHA. Because `plugin.json` pins `1.0.0` and it never changed, Claude Code sees the same version and serves the cache — users get nothing. Distractor 1 is the rule ONLY when `version` is omitted on git sources; here it is set, so the SHA fallback never triggers. Distractor 3 is wrong because even after a marketplace update the version string is still `1.0.0`. Distractor 4 invents an error that doesn't occur. The fix is to omit `version` for fast-iterating internal plugins or bump it every release.
options: The latest commit — git-based sources always treat each new commit as a new version.
Nothing — Claude Code resolves the version from `plugin.json` first, sees the unchanged `1.0.0`, and keeps the cached copy.
The latest commit, but only if they also run `/plugin marketplace update` first.
An error — a stale `version` in `plugin.json` against newer commits is a validation failure.

stage-6.prompt: Using the four-tier decision rule (ask the questions in order, first 'yes' wins), select EVERY item that correctly belongs in the tier given.
stage-6.explanation: The decision rule runs top-down: org-must-enforce → Tier 3; reused across repos → Tier 2; one repo → Tier 1; else → Tier 4. Options 1-3 apply it correctly. Option 4 is wrong: personal theme/model preferences are Tier 4 (personal, local-only `~/.claude` / `~/.codex`), not Tier 1 — a personal preference doesn't become repo config just because it affects a session. Option 5 is wrong: a mandatory org version floor is a non-negotiable guardrail → Tier 3 managed settings; marketplace plugin versioning is unrelated to enforcing a client version floor.
options: A mandatory rule that only an org-approved list of MCP servers may run → Tier 3 (managed settings).
A `run-migrations` skill used only by the `my-service` repo → Tier 1 (project config in `.claude/`).
A `code-review` skill reused across a dozen repos by the platform team → Tier 2 (marketplace plugin).
An engineer's personal preference for a dark theme and a specific model → Tier 1, because it changes how the repo's agent behaves.
A version floor requiring everyone to run at least Claude Code vX → Tier 2, because version management is what marketplaces do.

stage-6.prompt: A plugin's `code-review/SKILL.md` worked when developed locally, but after a teammate installs it via the marketplace, a hook in the plugin fails because it can't find `../shared-utils/lint.sh`, and even an in-plugin reference like `./scripts/check.sh` resolves to the wrong place. What's the underlying cause?
stage-6.explanation: Plugins are copied into `~/.claude/plugins/cache/<marketplace>/<plugin>/<version>/`, so (a) files outside the plugin directory like `../shared-utils` are never copied, and (b) relative-to-cwd paths break — you must reference in-plugin files with `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}`. Distractor 1 is the opposite of the rule: only the manifest goes in `.claude-plugin/`; component dirs sit at the plugin root. Distractor 3 invents a sandbox. Distractor 4 is a freshness issue, not the path-resolution cause described.
options: The plugin manifest is in the wrong location — `plugin.json` and the component dirs must all live inside `.claude-plugin/`.
On install the plugin is copied into a per-user versioned cache, so paths outside the plugin dir aren't copied and cwd-relative paths don't resolve; in-plugin paths must use `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}`.
Marketplace plugins run in a sandbox that blocks all filesystem access except read-only manifests.
The teammate forgot to run `/plugin marketplace update`, so they got a stale copy of the scripts.

stage-6.prompt: You want a freshly cloned repo to auto-prompt new hires to install exactly the team's plugins, and you want CI containers to start fully provisioned with zero network clones at runtime. Which statements are correct?
stage-6.explanation: Option 1 is the Tier-1/Tier-2 bridge: the marketplace lives in Tier 2 but the declaration that THIS repo uses it is committed project config. Option 3 correctly describes the seed dir (read-only, auto-update disabled, precedence at startup, mirrors `~/.claude/plugins`). Option 4 correctly describes `enabledPlugins`. Option 2 is wrong: marketplace state is PER-USER in `~/.claude/plugins/known_marketplaces.json`, not per-project — that's precisely why the committed declaration + trust-prompt mechanism (and seeding) are needed. Option 5 is wrong: `--scope project` writes into the committed `.claude/settings.json`, NOT the per-user default.
options: The `extraKnownMarketplaces` + `enabledPlugins` declaration goes in the committed `.claude/settings.json` (Tier-1 project config), even though the marketplace itself lives in Tier 2.
Marketplace registrations are stored per-project inside `.claude/`, so committing the repo is sufficient to share them with no per-user state.
For containers/CI, set `CLAUDE_CODE_PLUGIN_SEED_DIR` to a pre-built mirror of `~/.claude/plugins`; the seed is read-only, has auto-updates disabled, and its entries take precedence at startup.
`enabledPlugins` controls which declared plugins are on by default after the repo is trusted.
Using `claude plugin marketplace add ... --scope project` writes the marketplace declaration into the per-user `~/.claude` config so every project inherits it.

stage-6.prompt: A plugin lives in a subdirectory `tools/deploy-plugin` of a large monorepo on GitLab, and you want reproducible installs that survive the branch being deleted later. Which marketplace `source` entry is correct?
stage-6.explanation: `git-subdir` is the source type for a plugin in a monorepo subdir (sparse clone), and pinning with `sha` gives reproducibility that survives a deleted branch on GitLab. Distractor 1 misuses `github`, which has no `path` field for subdirs. Distractor 3 is wrong because a relative path resolves from the MARKETPLACE root, not an arbitrary external monorepo — it only works when the plugin is in the same marketplace repo. Distractor 4 uses `url` (whole-repo git) with a `path` field it doesn't support; `url` clones the whole repo, not a subdir.
options: `{ "source": "github", "repo": "org/monorepo", "path": "tools/deploy-plugin" }`
`{ "source": "git-subdir", "url": "https://gitlab.com/org/monorepo.git", "path": "tools/deploy-plugin", "sha": "<40-char>" }`
`"./tools/deploy-plugin"` — a relative path, since the plugin is in a subdirectory.
`{ "source": "url", "url": "https://gitlab.com/org/monorepo.git", "path": "tools/deploy-plugin" }`

stage-6.prompt: A team sets `"version": "2.0.0"` in BOTH `plugin.json` and the plugin's marketplace entry, then later bumps ONLY the marketplace entry to `2.1.0` to ship a fix. Users don't get the fix. Why?
stage-6.explanation: Resolution order is `plugin.json` first, then the marketplace entry, then SHA. With both set, `plugin.json`'s `2.0.0` wins SILENTLY and masks the marketplace `2.1.0` — users stay on the cached version. That's exactly why the guidance says never set `version` in both places. Distractor 1 is false: the marketplace entry is the #2 resolver when `plugin.json` lacks a version. Distractor 3 invents a blocking error. Distractor 4 invents a dependency on `validate`, which checks structure, not version precedence.
options: Marketplace-entry versions are advisory only and never affect resolution; only `plugin.json` and git SHA count.
`plugin.json`'s version wins silently over the marketplace entry, so it still resolves to `2.0.0` and the cache is kept.
Setting `version` in two places is a hard validation error that blocks the update entirely.
The marketplace entry only takes effect after running `claude plugin validate .`, which they skipped.
